content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

It was a Tuesday morning and I was in the middle of a prompt.
Nothing dramatic. I was asking ChatGPT to help me restructure a product description. Midway through reading the response, something appeared at the bottom of the screen that had never been there before. A small banner. Sponsored content. An ad inside the tool I was paying attention to, inside the thinking space I had been using every morning for over a year.
I closed the tab.
Not out of anger. More out of a quiet recognition that something had shifted in a way I had not expected and did not want to adapt to. I had been using ChatGPT since early 2024. It had become part of the daily rhythm of how I worked — first draft help, research, stuck moments, ideation. I was not looking to leave. I had not been comparing alternatives. I was genuinely comfortable with where things were.
That ad changed something. Not because it was intrusive. Because of what it meant.
A tool that shows you ads has made a decision about what you are. You are no longer a user. You are inventory. The product being sold to the advertiser is your attention, and the tool’s job is now split between helping you and monetizing you. Those two things are not always in conflict. But they are never fully aligned either.
I opened Claude that afternoon.

I had used Claude before, briefly, the way most people use the second option — when the first one was slow, or when I was curious, or when someone shared a link and I tried it once and forgot about it. I did not have strong feelings about it. It was fine. Different. Not obviously better or worse in any way I had taken time to examine.
This time I decided to actually pay attention.
The first thing I noticed was the writing. Not that it was more impressive technically. That it sounded different. Less like a tool generating output and more like something that had actually read what I wrote before responding to it. I gave it a draft I had been stuck on for three days. It came back with edits that felt considered rather than automatic. Small word choices that were better without being showy. A structural suggestion that I had not thought of and immediately recognized as correct.
I kept going.
I gave it my worst prompt — vague, half-formed, the kind of thing I would normally spend ten minutes refining before submitting. Claude asked a clarifying question instead of guessing. One question. Specific. The answer to it unlocked the whole thing in under two minutes.
That had never happened with ChatGPT. ChatGPT would attempt the vague prompt. Sometimes it would land. More often it would give me something I had to redirect three or four times before it became useful. Claude just stopped and asked what it needed to know.
That small difference compounded over sixty days into something significant.

By week three I had stopped going back.
Not because I made a decision. Because I kept reaching for Claude first and it kept being the right call. Writing tasks, stuck creative moments, structuring arguments, reviewing my own drafts — Claude was more consistent in a way that is hard to quantify but easy to feel after enough repetitions.
The output sounded less like AI. That is the clearest way I can describe it. When I edited Claude’s drafts, I was making them mine. When I edited ChatGPT’s drafts, I was often rewriting them. The starting point was different, and the distance from that starting point to something publishable was consistently shorter with Claude.
There were things ChatGPT did better. Image generation — Claude does not do this, and there were moments I missed it. Speed on quick lookups — ChatGPT’s browsing felt faster for simple current-data questions. If I needed a rough brainstorm of twenty ideas in thirty seconds, ChatGPT’s willingness to throw everything at once was occasionally useful.
But those were edge cases in my actual workflow. The core of what I do every day — writing, thinking through problems, building digital products, structuring content — Claude handled all of it better. Not dramatically. Consistently. And in knowledge work, consistent beats dramatic every time.

The ad thing stayed with me throughout.
Not as resentment. More as a lens. Once you see that a tool has split its loyalty, you start noticing the small ways that split shows up. The responses that feel slightly optimized for engagement rather than accuracy. The suggested follow-ups that extend the conversation rather than resolve it. The interface choices that keep you inside the product longer than necessary.
I am not saying ChatGPT is doing this deliberately or badly. I am saying the incentive structure changed the day ads entered the product, and incentive structures matter more than intentions over time.
Claude’s free tier is more limited in message volume. The paid version costs the same as ChatGPT Plus. There are no ads on either tier. The tool’s only job is to be useful to the person using it. That is not a small thing. That is the entire thing.

Sixty days in, I use Claude for everything I used to use ChatGPT for, and the work is better. Not unrecognizably better. Just consistently, quietly, noticeably better.
I still have ChatGPT open in another tab. I reach for it maybe once a week for image generation or a quick web lookup where speed matters more than depth.
But the serious work — the writing, the thinking, the building — that all happens in Claude now.
The ad was not the reason. It was just the moment I finally paid attention to a switch I should have made earlier.
